statement: Vue 3's reactivity system is built on ES2015 Proxy objects rather than Object.defineProperty (Vue 2). This means Vue 3 can detect property additions, deletions, and array index mutations natively — all of which required workarounds ($set, $delete, splice) in Vue 2. The Proxy wraps the entire object and intercepts get/set traps, enabling deep automatic tracking without explicit declaration of every observed key. The trade-off is that Proxy is not polyfillable, which is why Vue 3 dropped IE11 support.

Options API organizes code by option type (data, methods, computed, watch) which co-locates Vue machinery but scatters logic for a single feature across multiple sections. Composition API organizes by logical concern: all code for a feature lives together in a composable function. The philosophical shift is from 'answer Vue's structural questions' to 'write logic, then expose it to Vue'. Composition API also enables full TypeScript inference without decorators and makes logic reuse trivial (extract a useX() function) instead of relying on mixins, which had implicit dependency and namespace-collision problems.

The Single-File Component (.vue file) is Vue's fundamental unit of encapsulation, co-locating template, script, and scoped styles in one file. This is not a cosmetic choice: the SFC compiler is what enables scoped CSS (adding data-v-xxxx attributes), script setup macro transformations, and block-level TypeScript inference. The SFC boundary defines the component's identity at build time, allowing tree-shaking, hot module replacement, and tooling (Volar, vue-loader) to reason about the component holistically rather than as separate JS/CSS/HTML files that happen to be linked.

Destructuring a reactive() object loses reactivity. When you write `const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 })`, count is now a plain number — the Proxy binding is severed. The same trap applies to function parameters and return values. The fix is either (a) keep accessing via the reactive object (state.count), (b) use toRefs() to convert all properties to individual ref() wrappers before destructuring, or (c) use ref() directly instead of reactive() for values you intend to destructure. This is one of the most frequent Vue 3 migration bugs and the primary reason many teams default to ref() for everything.

ref() wraps primitive values in a { value } container so the Proxy can track mutations. Inside <script setup>, refs auto-unwrap in the template — you write {{ count }} not {{ count.value }}. But inside JavaScript (setup function body, composables, watchers) you must always use count.value. Forgetting .value produces silent bugs: the assignment mutates the raw primitive and is not tracked. A secondary trap: reactive() auto-unwraps nested refs — reading state.myRef gives the unwrapped value — which can make behavior inconsistent depending on how deeply a ref is nested. The mental model requires distinguishing template-land from JS-land at all times.

Pinia is Vue's official recommended state manager as of Vue 3, superseding Vuex. The key differences: Pinia has no mutations — state is mutated directly in actions (or even outside them for simple cases), eliminating the action→mutation ceremony Vuex required. Stores are defined as plain functions returning state/getters/actions, giving full TypeScript inference without plugin hacks. Pinia uses one store-per-feature rather than one giant tree, avoiding module namespace collisions. For Vuex 4→Pinia migrations, the mapping is: state→state, getters→getters, actions+mutations→actions. Vuex 5 was abandoned in favor of Pinia, so Vuex is effectively in maintenance mode.

Vue Router navigation guards run in a strict order: global beforeEach → per-route beforeEnter → component beforeRouteEnter. Guards must call next() (Options API) or return a value (Composition API with onBeforeRouteUpdate). The most common patterns: auth gates in global beforeEach checking a Pinia store, per-route data prefetching in beforeEnter to ensure data is ready before the component mounts, and component-level beforeRouteLeave to warn on unsaved changes. A subtle trap: beforeRouteEnter is the only guard that cannot access `this` because the component hasn't mounted yet — use the callback form of next() to get the component instance, or fetch via the store instead.

Plain Vue is a client-side SPA framework: the server sends an empty HTML shell and JS hydrates the page. Nuxt wraps Vue with a full-stack opinionated layer: file-system routing (pages/ directory), multiple rendering modes (SSR, SSG, SPA, hybrid per-route), server-side API routes (server/api/), and automatic data-fetching composables (useAsyncData, useFetch) that run on the server and hydrate on the client. The architectural choice is: choose plain Vue when you control the routing/rendering separately (e.g., embedded in a Laravel app, or an internal dashboard), choose Nuxt when you own the full stack and want SEO, TTFB, or universal data-fetching. Nuxt's trade-off is a larger abstraction surface and stricter file conventions.

<script setup> is syntactic sugar compiled by the SFC compiler to the Composition API's setup() function, but with key ergonomic differences: top-level declarations (refs, composables, imported components) are automatically available in the template without explicit return {}; defineProps, defineEmits, defineExpose are compiler macros (not runtime imports) that emit the correct component options. Limitations to know: (1) you cannot use await at the top level without wrapping in an async setup — use Suspense for async components; (2) defineProps cannot reference types defined in external files in some TS configurations (requires explicit import); (3) the component has no name unless you add a second <script> block with `export default { name: 'MyComponent' }` or use a Vite plugin like vite-plugin-vue-setup-extend.

provide/inject is Vue's native dependency injection for prop-drilling avoidance across deeply nested component trees. A parent calls provide(key, value) and any descendant (not just direct child) calls inject(key) to receive it. In Vue 3 with Composition API, provide/inject is reactive-aware: if you provide a ref or reactive object, the injected value is live. Best practices: (1) provide from app-level (app.provide) for global concerns like theme or i18n; (2) use Symbol keys to avoid naming collisions in large apps; (3) prefer readonly() wrapping of provided state to enforce single-direction data flow; (4) do not use provide/inject as a replacement for Pinia — it lacks devtools integration and is harder to serialize. The pattern is ideal for component libraries where direct prop threading breaks encapsulation.
